summary: Joe Reis examines the tension between traditional BI and AI-powered BI, noting that while old-school dashboards will persist due to ingrained habits, BI adoption has stagnated at 25% for seven years. He also addresses widespread corporate burnout among tech workers, advising them to either leverage their position for personal gain or exit the industry entirely.
key_points: Old-school BI will persist due to the Lindy Effect and ingrained user behaviors
BI adoption has remained stagnant at 25% for seven years
AI BI's real value lies in agent workflows that take action, not just generate insights
Poor data quality will prevent fully automated bot-run companies for the foreseeable future
Tech workers are experiencing severe burnout and questioning their career paths
money_quotes: The percentage of employees actively using BI/analytics tools is currently 25% on average, reflecting minimal growth in the past seven years we've been tracking this metric.
Insights don't mean much if they're not executed (although doing nothing is sometimes a wise move).
Given the poor data quality in most companies, I don't see this happening for a while (unless bots can also fix the underlying data).
Your Big Company very likely doesn't care about you. If you're an employee at a Big Company and in the rat race, here's my take - be greedy.
There is a life outside of data and tech, and I know many people who left the rat race and are FAR happier with their lives.
key_insight: Despite AI BI hype, the fundamental challenge remains unchanged: BI adoption has flatlined for years, and flashy new technology won't fix the deeper organizational and data quality problems that prevent analytics from driving real action.
